does: This paper presents Moirain, a suite of neural models for generating RNA sequences that interact with specific target proteins. The approach employs a three-stage pipeline: pretraining on large-scale RNA corpora to learn sequence plausibility, multimodal supervised fine-tuning that conditions RNA generation on protein structural and sequential features, and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to refine functional fitness using synthetic interaction data. The key technical contribution lies in applying DPO—which can handle non-aligned preference spaces—to improve the authenticity and interaction frequency of generated RNA sequences without requiring gold-standard alignment, addressing limitations of previous language modeling and protein design approaches.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in conditional sequence generation and alignment methods in computational biology. It extends natural language modeling techniques to the RNA domain, drawing on successes in protein design and deep learning-based sequence synthesis. The use of multimodal conditioning connects to prior work on protein-structure-aware sequence generation, while the application of Direct Preference Optimization adapts alignment techniques from large language model training to the RNA domain. The paper responds to the recognized gap between sequence generation capability and functional RNA design, combining ideas from pretraining, fine-tuning, and preference-based optimization that have proven effective in related sequence design tasks.
enables: This work opens pathways for improving RNA therapeutics and synthetic biology applications by demonstrating how preference-based optimization can enhance functional RNA design without requiring perfectly aligned training data. The multimodal alignment framework could generalize to other molecular design problems combining sequence and structural constraints. Future work could extend DPO-based refinement to RNA-RNA interactions, explore applications in vaccine design and gene therapy, and investigate how synthetic preference data generation can be scaled and improved. The approach also suggests promising directions for combining foundation models with domain-specific alignment techniques in molecular discovery, potentially enabling more efficient exploration of functional sequence spaces for therapeutic and biotechnology applications.
